title: Integration using completing the square
description: Find indefinite integrals of rational functions of the form 1/p(x) or 1/ŌłÜp(x), where p(x) is a quadratic function, by completing the square in p(x).
topic_path: Integrating functions using long division and completing the square

hints:
The [[Ōśā definition 1]] is in the form $\dfrac{1}{p(x)}$ where $p(x)$ is a quadratic expression. This suggests that we should rewrite $p(x)$ by completing the square.

Specifically, we will rewrite $p(x)$ as $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$. Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of the inverse tangent function:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD k} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD h}{\maroonD k}\right)+C$

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$:

$\begin{align}
x^2-6x+13&=x^2-6x+9+4
\\\\
&=(x-3)^2+4
\\\\
&=(x\blueD{-3})^2+\maroonD{2}^2
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{x^2-6x+13}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x\blueD{-3})^2+\maroonD{2}^2}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{2}} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x\blueD{-3}}{\maroonD{2}}\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{x^2-6x+13}\,dx=\dfrac12 \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x-3}{2}\right)+C$

The [[Ōśā definition 1]] is in the form $\dfrac{1}{p(x)}$ where $p(x)$ is a quadratic expression. This suggests that we should rewrite $p(x)$ by completing the square.

Specifically, we will rewrite $p(x)$ as $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$. Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of the inverse tangent function:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD k} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD h}{\maroonD k}\right)+C$

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$:

$\begin{align}
x^2+8x+52&=x^2+8x+16+36
\\\\
&=(x+4)^2+36
\\\\
&=(x+\blueD{4})^2+\maroonD{6}^2
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+8x+52}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x+\blueD{4})^2+\maroonD{6}^2}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{6}} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD{4}}{\maroonD{6}}\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+8x+52}\,dx=\dfrac16 \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+4}{6}\right)+C$

The [[Ōśā definition 1]] is in the form $\dfrac{1}{p(x)}$ where $p(x)$ is a quadratic expression. This suggests that we should rewrite $p(x)$ by completing the square.

Specifically, we will rewrite $p(x)$ as a constant multiple of $(x+h)^2+k^2$. Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of the inverse tangent function:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{\maroonD k} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD h}{\maroonD k}\right)+C$

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as a constant multiple of $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$:

$\begin{align}
2x^2-4x+20&=2[x^2-2x+10]
\\\\
&=2[x^2-2x+1+9]
\\\\
&=2[(x-1)^2+9]
\\\\
&=2[(x\blueD{-1})^2+\maroonD{3}^2]
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{2x^2-4x+20}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{2[(x-1)^2+3^2]}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac12\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x\blueD{-1})^2+\maroonD{3}^2}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac12\cdot\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{3}} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x\blueD{-1}}{\maroonD{3}}\right)+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac16 \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x-1}{3}\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{2x^2-4x+20}\,dx=\dfrac16 \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x-1}{3}\right)+C$

The [[Ōśā definition 1]] is in the form $\dfrac{1}{p(x)}$ where $p(x)$ is a quadratic expression. This suggests that we should rewrite $p(x)$ by completing the square.

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as a constant multiple of $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$:

$\begin{align}
6x^2+36x+78&=6[x^2+6x+13]
\\\\
&=6[x^2+6x+9+4]
\\\\
&=6[(x+3)^2+4]
\\\\
&=6[(x+\blueD{3})^2+\maroonD{2}^2]
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{6x^2+36x+78}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{6[(x+3)^2+2^2]}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac16\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x+\blueD{3})^2+\maroonD{2}^2}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac16\cdot\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{2}} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD{3}}{\maroonD{2}}\right)+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{12} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+3}{2}\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{6x^2+36x+78}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{12} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+3}{2}\right)+C$

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as a constant multiple of $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$:

$\begin{align}
5x^2-20x+100&=5[x^2-4x+20]
\\\\
&=5[x^2-4x+4+16]
\\\\
&=5[(x-2)^2+16]
\\\\
&=5[(x\blueD{-2})^2+\maroonD{4}^2]
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{5x^2-20x+100}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{5[(x-2)^2+4^2]}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac15\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x\blueD{-2})^2+\maroonD{4}^2}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac15\cdot\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{4}} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x\blueD{-2}}{\maroonD{4}}\right)+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{20} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x-2}{4}\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{5x^2-20x+100}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{20} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x-2}{4}\right)+C$

The [[Ōśā definition 1]] is in the form $\dfrac{1}{p(x)}$ where $p(x)$ is a quadratic expression. This suggests that we should rewrite $p(x)$ by completing the square.

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as a constant multiple of $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$:

$\begin{align}
4x^2+48x+148&=4[x^2+12x+37]
\\\\
&=4[x^2+12x+36+1]
\\\\
&=4[(x+6)^2+1]
\\\\
&=4[(x+\blueD{6})^2+\maroonD{1}^2]
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{4x^2+48x+148}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{4[(x+6)^2+1^2]}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac14\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x+\blueD{6})^2+\maroonD{1}^2}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac14\cdot\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{1}} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD{6}}{\maroonD{1}}\right)+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{4} \text{arctan}\left(x+6\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{4x^2+48x+148}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{4} \text{arctan}\left(x+6\right)+C$

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as a constant multiple of $(x+\blueD h)^2+\maroonD k^2$:

$\begin{align}
3x^2+6x+78&=3[x^2+2x+26]
\\\\
&=3[x^2+2x+1+25]
\\\\
&=3[(x+1)^2+25]
\\\\
&=3[(x+\blueD{1})^2+\maroonD{5}^2]
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{3x^2+6x+78}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{3[(x+1)^2+5^2]}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac13\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x+\blueD{1})^2+\maroonD{5}^2}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac13\cdot\dfrac{1}{\maroonD{5}} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD{1}}{\maroonD{5}}\right)+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{15} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+1}{5}\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{3x^2+6x+78}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{15} \text{arctan}\left(\dfrac{x+1}{5}\right)+C$

The [[Ōśā definition 1]] is in the form $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{p(x)}}$ where $p(x)$ is a quadratic expression. This suggests that we should rewrite $p(x)$ by completing the square.

Specifically, we will rewrite $p(x)$ as $\maroonD k^2-(x+\blueD h)^2$. Then, we will be able to integrate using our knowledge of the derivative of the inverse sine function:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\maroonD k^2-x^2}}\,dx=\text{arcsin}\left(\dfrac{x}{\maroonD k}\right)+C$

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

By setting $u=x+\blueD h$ and using $u$-substitution, we get the following formula:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\maroonD k^2-(x+\blueD h)^2}}\,dx=\text{arcsin}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD h}{\maroonD k}\right)+C$

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as $\maroonD k^2-(x+\blueD h)^2$:

$\begin{align}
-x^2-4x+21&=21-(x^2+4x)
\\\\
&=21+4-(x^2+4x+4)
\\\\
&=25-(x+2)^2
\\\\
&=\maroonD{5}^2-(x+\blueD{2})^2
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{-x^2-4x+21}}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\maroonD{5}^2-(x+\blueD{2})^2}}\,dx
\\\\
&=\text{arcsin}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD{2}}{\maroonD{5}}\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{-x^2-4x+21}}\,dx=\text{arcsin}\left(\dfrac{x+2}{5}\right)+C$

We start by rewriting $p(x)$ as $\maroonD k^2-(x+\blueD h)^2$:

$\begin{align}
-x^2-14x-48&=-48-(x^2+14x)
\\\\
&=-48+49-(x^2+14x+49)
\\\\
&=1-(x+7)^2
\\\\
&=\maroonD{1}^2-(x+\blueD{7})^2
\end{align}$

Now we can find the integral:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{-x^2-14x-48}}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\maroonD{1}^2-(x+\blueD{7})^2}}\,dx
\\\\
&=\text{arcsin}\left(\dfrac{x+\blueD{7}}{\maroonD{1}}\right)+C
\\\\
&=\text{arcsin}\left(x+7\right)+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{-x^2-14x-48}}\,dx=\text{arcsin}\left(x+7\right)+C$
